## HTML Document Structure

Every web page begins its life as an HTML document. HTML, or HyperText Markup Language, is the foundational language of the web. It does not control how a page looks or behaves --- that is the job of CSS and JavaScript --- but it defines the content and structure that everything else builds upon. Understanding HTML document structure is the first step toward becoming a competent web developer, and the concepts here will follow you throughout your entire career.

### The DOCTYPE Declaration

## Text & Links

Text content and hyperlinks are the core of the web. Before images, animations, and interactive applications, the web was fundamentally a system of documents linked together. Understanding how HTML handles text formatting and linking is essential because these elements appear on virtually every web page ever created.

## Images & Media

The web would be a dull place without images, audio, and video. HTML provides dedicated elements for embedding media, and understanding how to use them properly is critical for creating accessible, performant web pages. Media elements are often the heaviest resources on a page, so getting them right has a significant impact on load times and user experience.

## Forms & Tables

Forms and tables are two of HTML's most powerful and complex element groups. Forms enable user interaction --- logins, searches, surveys, e-commerce checkouts --- making them central to any dynamic website. Tables organize data into rows and columns. Both have rich attribute systems and accessibility requirements that many developers get wrong.

web-css-1:
## Selectors & Properties

CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the language that transforms raw HTML structure into visual design. While HTML defines what content appears on a page, CSS controls how that content looks --- colors, fonts, spacing, layout, animations, and every other visual aspect. Understanding selectors and properties is fundamental because they form the mechanism by which CSS targets HTML elements and applies styles.

### CSS Selectors

Selectors are patterns that identify which HTML elements a rule applies to. The simplest selectors target elements by type (\
web-css-2:
## Box Model

Every element on a web page is represented as a rectangular box. The CSS box model defines the structure of this box and how its dimensions are calculated. Misunderstanding the box model is one of the most common sources of layout bugs for beginning web developers, and a solid grasp of it is essential for creating predictable, consistent layouts.

### The Four Layers

The box model consists of four concentric areas, from inside to outside: content, padding, border, and margin. The content area holds the element's text, images, or child elements. Padding is the space between the content and the border --- it creates breathing room inside the element and takes on the element's background color. The border sits between the padding and the margin and can be styled with width, style (solid, dashed, dotted, etc.), and color. The margin is the space outside the border that separates the element from its neighbors --- margins are always transparent.

### Content-Box vs. Border-Box

## Colors & Typography

Color and typography are the two most impactful visual design tools in CSS. The right color palette creates mood, directs attention, and establishes brand identity. Good typography ensures readability and creates visual hierarchy. Together, they can elevate a basic HTML page from an amateur exercise to a professional-looking website.

## Positioning & Layout

CSS positioning determines where elements appear on the page and how they interact with surrounding elements. The positioning system in CSS can be confusing because different position values fundamentally change the rules for how an element is placed. Mastering positioning is essential for creating overlapping elements, fixed navigation bars, tooltips, modals, and countless other common UI patterns.

## Flexbox

Flexbox (Flexible Box Layout) revolutionized CSS layout when it achieved broad browser support around 2015. Before Flexbox, developers relied on floats, inline-block hacks, and table-based layouts to achieve even basic arrangements like centering an element or creating equal-height columns. Flexbox provides a one-dimensional layout model that excels at distributing space and aligning items along a single axis --- either horizontally or vertically.

### The Flex Container

web-adv-2:
## CSS Grid

CSS Grid is a two-dimensional layout system that allows you to control both rows and columns simultaneously. While Flexbox excels at distributing items along a single axis, Grid was designed for laying out entire page structures --- headers, sidebars, content areas, and footers --- all at once. Grid and Flexbox are complementary: use Grid for the overall page layout and Flexbox for alignment within individual components.

## Responsive Design

Responsive design is the practice of building websites that adapt gracefully to any screen size, from small phones to ultrawide monitors. With mobile devices accounting for over half of global web traffic, responsive design is not optional --- it is a fundamental requirement. A responsive website provides the same content and functionality across devices while optimizing the layout and interaction patterns for each screen size.

## Variables & Types

JavaScript is the programming language of the web. While HTML provides structure and CSS provides style, JavaScript provides behavior --- interactivity, dynamic content updates, form validation, animations, and communication with servers. Understanding variables and data types is the absolute foundation of JavaScript programming, and getting these fundamentals right makes everything else easier.

### Variable Declarations

## Functions & Scope

Functions are the fundamental building blocks of JavaScript programs. They encapsulate reusable logic, organize code into manageable pieces, and create the scope boundaries that control variable access. Understanding functions deeply --- including how they create scope, how closures work, and how JavaScript's execution model processes them --- is essential for writing clean, bug-free code.

## Arrays & Objects

Arrays and objects are the two primary data structures in JavaScript. Together, they can represent virtually any data shape you will encounter in web development --- lists of items, user profiles, API responses, application state, and configuration settings. Mastering the methods available on these structures is what separates productive JavaScript developers from beginners who write verbose, error-prone loops for everything.

## DOM Manipulation

The Document Object Model (DOM) is the browser's representation of an HTML document as a tree of objects. JavaScript interacts with the web page through the DOM, reading content, modifying elements, responding to user actions, and dynamically creating new content. Understanding DOM manipulation is what transforms a static HTML page into an interactive web application.

### Selecting Elements

Before you can manipulate an element, you need to select it. Modern JavaScript provides two primary selection methods. \
web-jsa-1:
## Async JavaScript

JavaScript is single-threaded, meaning it can only execute one piece of code at a time. Yet modern web applications constantly perform operations that take time --- fetching data from servers, reading files, waiting for user input, running timers. Asynchronous programming allows JavaScript to initiate these operations and continue executing other code while waiting for them to complete, without blocking the user interface.

### The Event Loop

JavaScript's concurrency model is based on the event loop. When JavaScript encounters an asynchronous operation (like a network request), it delegates the work to the browser's Web APIs and continues executing the next line of code. When the operation completes, its callback is placed in a task queue. The event loop continuously checks whether the call stack (where currently executing code runs) is empty, and if so, it moves the next callback from the queue to the call stack for execution. ECMAScript 2015 (ES6) and subsequent versions introduced transformative features that modernized JavaScript. These features are not just syntactic sugar --- they solve real problems with the language, improve code readability, and enable patterns that were previously difficult or impossible. Every modern JavaScript codebase uses these features extensively, making them essential knowledge for web developers.

web-jsa-3:
## APIs & JSON

APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are the mechanism through which web applications communicate with servers, third-party services, and other software components. Understanding how to work with APIs is essential for any web developer because virtually every modern web application depends on server communication for data, authentication, and functionality. JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the near-universal format for this communication.

### What is a REST API?

REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing APIs. A REST API exposes resources (data entities like users, posts, products) at URL endpoints and uses standard HTTP methods to operate on them: GET retrieves a resource, POST creates a new resource, PUT replaces a resource entirely, PATCH partially updates a resource, and DELETE removes a resource. web-react-1:
## Components & JSX

React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces. Created by Facebook (now Meta) and released in 2013, it introduced a component-based architecture that revolutionized front-end development. Rather than manipulating the DOM directly, React lets you describe what the UI should look like as a function of data, and it efficiently updates the DOM to match. Understanding components and JSX --- React's fundamental building blocks --- is the gateway to the entire React ecosystem.

### What Are Components?

A component is a self-contained, reusable piece of UI. Think of a web page as composed of nested components: a Header component containing a Logo component and a Navigation component, a Main component containing ArticleCard components, and a Footer component. Each component manages its own structure, appearance, and behavior. This decomposition makes complex UIs manageable because you can develop, test, and reason about each piece independently. Components can be as small as a button or as large as an entire page. The key principle is that each component should have a single responsibility --- it should do one thing and do it well.

### Functional Components

Modern React uses functional components exclusively (class components are legacy). A functional component is a JavaScript function that accepts a \
web-react-2:
## State & Hooks

State is data that changes over time and affects what a component renders. While props come from parents and are read-only, state is owned and managed by the component itself. When state changes, React automatically re-renders the component and its children to reflect the new data. Hooks are functions that let you use state and other React features in functional components. Understanding state management and hooks is central to building interactive React applications.

## Routing & Forms

Single-page applications (SPAs) need routing to display different views based on the URL without full page reloads, and forms to collect user input. React Router is the standard routing library for React, and React provides powerful patterns for handling forms. Together, these enable building complete, multi-page applications within the React framework.

### React Router Basics

React Router enables client-side routing --- navigating between views by changing the URL and rendering different components, all without requesting new pages from the server. Install with \

## React Patterns

As React applications grow in complexity, patterns emerge for solving recurring problems --- sharing state between components, conditionally rendering UI, managing lists, and organizing application logic. Understanding these patterns is what separates developers who can build small demos from those who can architect large, maintainable applications.

### Context API

The Context API solves the "prop drilling" problem --- when data needs to pass through many intermediate components that do not use it themselves, just to reach a deeply nested component that does. web-back-1:
## Node.js & Express

Node.js brought JavaScript to the server, enabling full-stack JavaScript development where the same language is used on both the client and server. Express is the most popular Node.js web framework, providing a minimal and flexible foundation for building web servers and APIs. Together, they power a significant portion of the web's backend infrastructure.

### What is Node.js?

Node.js is a JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine. It allows JavaScript to run outside the browser, with access to the file system, network, and operating system. Node.js uses an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model, meaning it handles concurrent operations efficiently without creating a new thread for each request (unlike traditional servers like Apache). This architecture makes Node.js particularly well-suited for I/O-intensive applications: APIs, real-time applications (chat, live dashboards), streaming services, and microservices. Node.js comes with npm (Node Package Manager), the world's largest software registry, providing access to over a million open-source packages. A \

## REST API Design

Designing a good REST API is as important as implementing one. A well-designed API is intuitive to use, consistent in its conventions, easy to extend without breaking existing clients, and well-documented. Poor API design leads to confused developers, frequent breaking changes, and client-side workarounds that accumulate as technical debt.

### RESTful Resource Design

REST APIs are organized around resources --- the nouns in your domain. Users, products, orders, and comments are resources. URLs should identify resources, not actions: \
web-back-3:
## Authentication & Security

Security is not a feature to add later --- it is a fundamental requirement from the first line of code. Web applications handle sensitive data (passwords, personal information, financial data), and security vulnerabilities can result in data breaches, financial loss, legal liability, and destroyed user trust. Authentication (verifying identity) and authorization (verifying permissions) are the gatekeepers of every web application.

### Password Hashing

Never store passwords in plain text. If your database is compromised, plain text passwords are immediately usable by attackers, and since many users reuse passwords across sites, a single breach can cascade. Passwords must be hashed using a slow, salted algorithm designed specifically for password storage. bcrypt is the industry standard: it automatically generates a random salt (preventing rainbow table attacks), and its work factor can be increased as hardware gets faster. ## SQL Fundamentals

Databases are where web applications store and retrieve persistent data. SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for interacting with relational databases --- systems like PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and SQL Server that store data in tables with defined schemas. Understanding SQL is essential for backend development because almost every web application needs to store, query, and manipulate data.

web-db-2:
## Database Design

Database design determines how data is organized into tables, what relationships exist between them, and what constraints maintain data integrity. Good database design minimizes redundancy, prevents anomalies (inconsistencies during insert/update/delete), and supports efficient querying. Poor design leads to data corruption, performance problems, and applications that are difficult to extend.

### Tables and Columns

A table represents a type of entity (users, products, orders). Each row is an instance of that entity, and each column is an attribute. Columns have data types that constrain what values they can hold: integers (\
web-db-3:
## NoSQL & MongoDB

Not all data fits neatly into tables with predefined schemas. NoSQL (Not Only SQL) databases emerged to handle use cases where relational databases struggle: highly variable data structures, massive horizontal scalability, rapid development cycles, and data that is naturally hierarchical or document-oriented. MongoDB is the most popular document database and represents the NoSQL approach that most web developers encounter first.

### Why NoSQL?

Relational databases require a fixed schema --- every row in a table must have the same columns. This works well for structured data (users, orders, financial records) but becomes awkward when data structures vary significantly. Consider a product catalog where clothing has sizes and colors, electronics have specifications and compatibility, and books have authors and ISBNs. In a relational database, you either create a sparse table with hundreds of mostly-null columns, use an Entity-Attribute-Value pattern (complex and slow), or create separate tables per product type (inflexible). Document databases let each document have its own structure, naturally accommodating this variability. NoSQL databases also typically scale horizontally (adding more servers) rather than vertically (making one server bigger), which is important for applications with massive data volumes or traffic.

### Document Model

MongoDB stores data as documents --- JSON-like objects (technically BSON, Binary JSON) organized into collections. A collection is analogous to a table, and a document is analogous to a row, but with key differences. Documents in the same collection can have different fields. Documents can contain nested objects and arrays, eliminating many join operations. A user document might look like: \
